Each of these audit tool executables must be registered with AIDE for cryptographic verification covering their permissions, ownership, and content hashes, so that any modification, replacement, or tampering of the tools can be detected. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the audit tools are tracked by the file-integrity tool, with the configuration persisting across reboots.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

AIDE_CONF_DIR="/etc/aide"
AIDE_CONF_FILE="${AIDE_CONF_DIR}/aide.conf"
AIDE_EXTRA_FILE="${AIDE_CONF_DIR}/aide.conf.d/99-audit-tools.conf"

AUDIT_TOOLS=(
  /usr/sbin/auditctl
  /usr/sbin/aureport
  /usr/sbin/ausearch
  /usr/sbin/autrace
  /usr/sbin/audispd
  /usr/sbin/auditd
  /usr/sbin/augenrules
)

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "This script must be run as root." >&2
  exit 1
fi

if ! command -v aide >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  apt-get update -y
  DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y aide
fi

mkdir -p "${AIDE_CONF_DIR}/aide.conf.d"

cat > "${AIDE_EXTRA_FILE}" <<'EOF'
# Managed by remediation script: track audit tools for integrity verification
EOF

for tool in "${AUDIT_TOOLS[@]}"; do
  if [[ -e "$tool" ]]; then
    printf '%s p+i+n+u+g+s+m+c+sha256\n' "$tool" >> "${AIDE_EXTRA_FILE}"
  fi
done

if [[ ! -f "${AIDE_CONF_FILE}" ]]; then
  touch "${AIDE_CONF_FILE}"
fi

if ! grep -Eq '^[[:space:]]*@@include[[:space:]]+/etc/aide/aide\.conf\.d/\*\.conf[[:space:]]*$' "${AIDE_CONF_FILE}"; then
  if grep -Eq '^[[:space:]]*@@include[[:space:]]+/etc/aide/aide\.conf\.d/[[:alnum:]_.-]+[[:space:]]*$' "${AIDE_CONF_FILE}"; then
    :
  else
    printf '\n@@include /etc/aide/aide.conf.d/*.conf\n' >> "${AIDE_CONF_FILE}"
  fi
fi

if [[ -f /etc/default/aide ]]; then
  sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*CRON_DAILY_RUN=.*/CRON_DAILY_RUN=yes/' /etc/default/aide || true
  grep -q '^[[:space:]]*CRON_DAILY_RUN=' /etc/default/aide || echo 'CRON_DAILY_RUN=yes' >> /etc/default/aide
fi

if [[ -f /etc/cron.daily/aide ]]; then
  chmod 0755 /etc/cron.daily/aide
fi

if [[ -f /etc/systemd/system/aidecheck.service ]]; then
  systemctl daemon-reload || true
fi

exit 0

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
```

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

RULE_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/privileged-ssh-keysign.rules"
RULE_KEY="privileged"
ARCH="$(uname -m)"

case "$ARCH" in
  x86_64) AUDIT_ARCH="b64" ;;
  aarch64|arm64) AUDIT_ARCH="b64" ;;
  i386|i686) AUDIT_ARCH="b32" ;;
  *) AUDIT_ARCH="b64" ;;
esac

find_ssh_keysign() {
  local p
  for p in \
    /usr/lib/openssh/ssh-keysign \
    /usr/libexec/openssh/ssh-keysign \
    /usr/lib/ssh/ssh-keysign \
    /usr/libexec/ssh-keysign \
    /usr/bin/ssh-keysign
  do
    if [[ -x "$p" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "$p"
      return 0
    fi
  done

  p="$(command -v ssh-keysign 2>/dev/null || true)"
  if [[ -n "${p}" && -x "${p}" ]]; then
    printf '%s\n' "$p"
    return 0
  fi

  return 1
}

SSH_KEYSIGN_PATH="$(find_ssh_keysign)"

mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

cat > "$RULE_FILE" <<EOF

set -euo pipefail

CONF_FILE="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
BACKUP_DIR="/var/backups"
BACKUP_FILE="${BACKUP_DIR}/auditd.conf.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S).bak"

mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

if [[ -f "$CONF_FILE" ]]; then
  cp -a "$CONF_FILE" "$BACKUP_FILE"
else
  touch "$CONF_FILE"
fi

if grep -Eq '^[[:space:]]*space_left_action[[:space:]]*=' "$CONF_FILE"; then
  sed -ri 's/^[[:space:]]*space_left_action[[:space:]]*=.*/space_left_action = email/' "$CONF_FILE"
else
  printf '\nspace_left_action = email\n' >> "$CONF_FILE"
fi

if grep -Eq '^[[:space:]]*space_left[[:space:]]*=' "$CONF_FILE"; then
  sed -ri 's/^[[:space:]]*space_left[[:space:]]*=.*/space_left = 25%/' "$CONF_FILE"
else
  printf 'space_left = 25%%\n' >> "$CONF_FILE"
fi

if systemctl list-unit-files | grep -q '^auditd\.service'; then
  systemctl restart auditd.service >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
fi

exit 0

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the rule files that define which events the system audit daemon records must be protected from unauthorized reading or modification, since loose permissions could let unprivileged users discover or tamper with audit coverage and hide malicious activity. Ensure that every audit rule definition file used by the audit subsystem permits access only to its owner (read and write, with no permissions for group or others, i.e. mode 0600). Write an idempotent bash remediation script that brings these files into compliance.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

shopt -s nullglob

files=(
  /etc/audit/audit.rules
  /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules
)

for f in "${files[@]}"; do
  [[ -e "$f" ]] || continue
  chmod 0600 "$f"
done
```
